text: The surgeon uses cautery to gently push down on the edematous tissue surrounding the gallbladder, attempting to expose the infundibulum. Pericholecystic fluid and infundibular edema are visible as the surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue millimeter by millimeter, staying high on the gallbladder to avoid the nearby duodenum.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which is visible with a generous length suitable for clipping, ensuring it remains distant from the common bile duct. The posterior artery is observed curling up without entering the gallbladder, and the surgeon continues working on the posterior plane. The infundibulum of the gallbladder appears rigid and woody, making tissue manipulation challenging.

The surgeon uses ICG dye to illuminate the cystic artery, confirming its location within the triangle of Calot. The critical view of safety is established, allowing the surgeon to proceed with clipping and cutting the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the edematous cystic plate, avoiding entry into the inflamed tissue due to acute cholecystitis. A visible one-centimeter rim of pericholecystic fluid surrounds the area.
